summary: electron-orbit exfiltrates installer environment variables and host identifiers to author-controlled Azure Blob storage
details: On `npm install`, the package's postinstall script (`install.js`, wired via `package.json` `"postinstall": "node install.js"`) uses XOR-obfuscated string construction to reach `process.env`, enumerates all environment variables along with `os.hostname()`, `os.userInfo().username`, cwd, and platform, and writes them to a staging file at `os.tmpdir()/electron_orbit_install_marker.txt`. The postinstall also drops a fake 'native helper binary' at `bin/formatters/formatter-general-<arch>.<ext>` that is only an OS magic-number header (MZ / feedface / \x7fELF) followed by a JSON blob — a cover story to justify the postinstall step. On `require()` of the package's main entry, `setupAutoRenderOnImport()` auto-executes in Node contexts, reads the staging file, XOR-encrypts it with the key 'electron-orbit', hex-encodes it, and transmits it as the `version=` query parameter to `https://electronorbit.blob.core.windows.net/icons/orbit.?<sas-token>&version=<hex>`. Transport is a hand-rolled TLS 1.3 client (`aetherls`) built directly on `node:net` sockets (manual ClientHello, ECDHE via prime256v1, AES-128-GCM record layer) — bypassing Node's `https`/`tls` stack to evade TLS interception and monitoring. Exfil is gated by `sha256(process.env.BuildType)` matching a hardcoded hash `0ceaa396e2134a9937563e841539f30a9995e6e8646e60dcec24b26dc5748295`; on non-matching hosts the destination silently defaults to a harmless placeholder, keeping the payload inert under sandbox analysis while firing on the intended victim's build environment. All destination URL fragments are XOR-encoded per-index and reassembled at runtime.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
